title: Federal Judge Rules ICE Warrantless Home Entry Violates Fourth Amendment
excerpt: U.S. District Judge Jeffrey M. Bryan ordered the immediate release of a Liberian national, finding that ICE violated both federal regulations and the Fourth Amendment when agents forcibly entered his home using only an administrative warrant. The ruling directly challenged ICE's secret 'Home Entry Memo' training directive.
content:
On January 17, 2026, U.S. District Judge Jeffrey M. Bryan issued a ruling finding that ICE violated the Fourth Amendment when agents forcibly entered the home of Garrison G., a Liberian national with no criminal record who had been consistently complying with all conditions of his supervised release, including GPS monitoring and regular ICE check-ins.

"This arrest violated the Fourth Amendment," Judge Bryan wrote, concluding that ICE agents "forcibly entered Garrison G.'s home without his consent and without a judicial warrant." The ruling came after agents used a battering ram to enter the home, armed with only a civil administrative deportation order — not a judicially-issued warrant.

  • The ruling is one of the first to directly strike down conduct authorized by ICE's secret internal "Home Entry Memo" (signed May 12, 2025 by Acting Director Todd Lyons)
  • The memo authorized ICE officers to use Form I-205 administrative forms — signed by DHS officials, not judges — as authority to forcibly enter residences
  • Whistleblowers later revealed new ICE recruits were being trained on the memo secretly, with instructors told not to document that training
